title: May inflation ticks up, defense exports target $20 billion, KILIÇ underwater drones debut at Efes
subhead: Turkey's May inflation crept back up to 32.6% annual, vindicating rate-hike calls. A Reuters analysis shows defense exports tripled since 2021 to $10 billion with eyes on doubling again. Aselsan's KILIÇ kamikaze underwater drones and Syria's first-ever participation in Efes 2026 signal Ankara's expanding military reach on and beneath the water.

sections.heading: May inflation edges up to 32.6%, keeping rate hike pressure alive
sections.body:
Turkey's annual inflation rate ticked up to 32.6% in May from 32.4% in April, official TUIK figures showed today, slightly above market expectations. On a monthly basis, consumer prices climbed 1.7%, a slowdown from April's 4.2% jump, but still driven primarily by housing, water costs, and electricity and gas — the energy pipeline that the Iran war keeps pressurizing.

The Istanbul Chamber of Commerce (ITO) measured the annual rate at 36.8% for Turkey's largest city, a persistent gap with official figures that underscores how unevenly price pressures are distributed. Turkey's annual inflation has remained above 30% since December 2021.

The central bank last month raised its year-end inflation forecast to 26% from 16%, explicitly citing elevated energy prices from the Middle East conflict. It predicted inflation would fall to 15% by end-2027 and 9% by 2028. But JPMorgan analysts said on May 22 that the central bank was likely to hike its key rate to 40% — from the current 37% — and could do so before its next scheduled June meeting, following politically-driven market turbulence and the lira hitting record lows.

Bloomberg reported on May 25 that investors are increasing bets on a rate hike, as higher energy costs keep inflation elevated and the political crisis adds lira pressure. The central bank has held at 37% for two consecutive meetings since the Iran war began in late February, warning that fallout could hit the inflation outlook.
sections.whyItMatters: The May print confirms that disinflation has stalled well above the central bank's own year-end target. With the policy rate at 37% and markets pricing in 40%, the next rate decision in late June will be the first since today's data — and the first since the CHP crisis sent the lira to record lows.

sections.heading: Defense exports tripled since 2021, Ankara targets $20 billion in two years
sections.body:
A Reuters analysis published today details how two decades of state investment have transformed Turkey into a major arms exporter. Turkish defense exports have more than tripled since 2021 to $10 billion last year, accounting for about 3.7% of total exports. Exports to Europe and the U.S. almost quadrupled over the same period to $5.6 billion.

The industry now supplies nearly 40 countries, mainly in the Gulf, Africa, Asia, and parts of Europe, with weapons that buyers see as cheaper, faster to deliver, and more adaptable than alternatives. Turkey supplies about 65% of armed drones used worldwide and is a major ammunition exporter. Baykar alone exported $2.2 billion in UAVs in 2025.

Turkey aims to double defense exports in two years, according to its defense agency. The product range is expanding: frigates, an aircraft carrier, air defense systems, armored vehicles, and fighter jets under development — Indonesia committed last year to buying 48 Turkish fighter jets.

The NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8 will serve as a showcase. Defense Minister Yaşar Güler urged European allies in April to abandon security arrangements that exclude non-EU NATO members, arguing such policies could harm Europe's security more than the U.S. troop reduction in Europe. NATO chief Mark Rutte said the planned defense industry forum would be the alliance's most comprehensive yet.

But hurdles remain. Turkey is largely excluded from the EU's SAFE programme, and some governments keep Ankara at arm's length over regional policy disputes and concerns about democratic backsliding. Even so, Turkish firms have secured deals with Poland, Spain, Portugal, and Romania, while Baykar acquired Italy's Piaggio Aerospace and partnered with Leonardo.

Can Kasapoglu, senior fellow at the Hudson Institute, said Turkey's defense industry had made a 'major leap' by exporting advanced systems, and that the Ukraine war underscored that modern warfare depends on industrial depth and sustainability — areas where Turkey has gained credibility. Defense analyst Arda Mevlutoğlu said conditions were in place for deeper cooperation with Europe if political obstacles can be managed.
sections.whyItMatters: The $10 billion figure and the doubling target are the most concrete numbers yet on Turkey's defense export trajectory. The NATO summit will be the moment Ankara tries to convert battlefield credibility and supply-chain speed into formal European partnerships — but EU political barriers around democratic governance won't yield to a single summit.

sections.heading: KILIÇ kamikaze underwater drones unveiled as Aselsan expands maritime portfolio
sections.body:
Aselsan showcased its KILIÇ series of kamikaze autonomous underwater vehicles at the Efes 2026 exercise, adding a new dimension to Turkey's unmanned naval capabilities. The drones, initially debuted at SAHA 2026, are designed to sense and strike maritime targets at ranges from 10 to 200 nautical miles, depending on the variant.

KILIÇ can be controlled through wire, RF, or satellite communications. While mostly submerged, elements protrude above water for communications and surface-vessel tracking with mounted sensors. Aselsan envisions swarm operations where multiple drones coordinate to sink vessels and deny vast stretches of water to enemy operations.

Naval News reports that recent global trends toward unmanned surface and underwater vehicles heavily influenced Aselsan's decision to develop KILIÇ. Aselsan stated: 'As maritime operations shift toward distributed, network-centric and increasingly asymmetric engagements, unmanned systems are emerging as a defining element of naval power.'

KILIÇ is the second kamikaze system developed by Aselsan designed to sink ships, after the Albatros Kamikaze USV. In 2023, a Turkish military drill sank a vessel in the Eastern Mediterranean using eight Albatros USVs and other unmanned systems. Concept graphics depict KILIÇ being launched from the coast and striking warships in port, or mounted on unmanned surface vessels.

For navies operating in constrained littoral environments, KILIÇ offers an asymmetrical area-denial solution at an affordable cost compared to traditional coastal defense cruise missiles — a capability directly relevant to Turkey's Aegean and Eastern Mediterranean posture.
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey already demonstrated swarm-boat warfare with Albatros. KILIÇ extends that concept underwater, adding a cheap area-denial layer that complements Turkey's surface and aerial drone dominance. For a country surrounded by constrained waterways — Aegean, Black Sea, Eastern Mediterranean — underwater kamikaze drones are a natural fit.

sections.heading: Efes 2026: Syria trains abroad for the first time, Libya sends unified 502-member delegation
sections.body:
The EFES-2026 exercise along Turkey's Aegean coast near Izmir drew around 10,000 troops from 50 countries, a record participation that highlighted Ankara's expanding military diplomacy. Two first-time participants stood out: Syria and Libya.

Syria sent a delegation of about 50 soldiers, marking the first time a Syrian unit representing the new Damascus leadership has taken part in an international military exercise, and the first instance of Syrian troops training abroad. According to Israel Defense, the participation is seen as a diplomatic and strategic achievement for Turkey, which has sought to normalize relations with Syria while maintaining influence along its southern border. Nine nations participated in Efes for the first time: Bulgaria, the Netherlands, Sweden, Japan, Egypt, Poland, Vietnam, Portugal, and Syria.

Libya's participation was even more striking. A 502-member military delegation drawn from multiple factions appeared under a unified banner with the slogan 'One Libya, One Army' — the first such unified appearance in an international exercise since Libya's internal conflict began. Israel Defense reports this reflects Turkish-backed efforts to consolidate Libya's fragmented armed forces. Libyan units trained in amphibious warfare, special forces tactics, combat diving, and explosive ordnance disposal.

Ankara also announced plans to expand military support to both Libya and Syria, particularly in training and operational planning, reinforcing Turkey's role as a key military patron for both states.
sections.whyItMatters: Syrian troops training on Turkish soil and Libyan factions appearing under a single banner at a Turkish-hosted exercise are tangible markers of Ankara's military reach into two Arab states in transition. Turkey is not just selling equipment — it is building interoperability and institutional ties that survive whichever government holds power in Tripoli or Damascus.

sections.heading: PKK peace process frozen, militant leadership says Ankara stalled on reforms
sections.body:
The PKK's most senior field commander, Murat Karayılan, said in an interview with the PKK-linked ANF outlet that the peace process is 'currently frozen,' contradicting President Erdoğan's claim that the process is 'proceeding as it should.' Karayılan said the PKK had fulfilled its responsibilities — including declaring a ceasefire and ending armed struggle — but that the Turkish government had failed to introduce the legislation promised by April.

A parliamentary committee recommended a series of reforms, including reintegration of PKK members who renounce violence, with legal steps tied to state verification of disarmament. No bill has been introduced. Karayılan said government officials had set April as the deadline for legislation — a deadline that has now passed.

Zagros Hiwa, spokesperson for the Kurdistan Communities Union (KCK), told the AP that Turkish forces continue to operate in northern Iraq, government-appointed administrators still occupy seats of elected Kurdish mayors, and thousands of political prisoners remain jailed. He accused Ankara of 'instrumentalizing' the process to consolidate the governing party's grip on power rather than seeking a genuine settlement.

Crucially, Karayılan said the PKK's 12th Congress decision to dissolve was approved on the condition that imprisoned leader Abdullah Öcalan personally manage the disarmament process — meaning the group's internal mandate cannot move forward while Öcalan remains in solitary confinement on İmralı island. Reuters reported on May 13 that the Iran war has brought the peace process to a near standstill, with both sides hesitating on next steps amid regional instability.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between Erdoğan's 'everything is fine' and the PKK's 'nothing is moving' is wide and getting wider. The Iran war gave Ankara a reason to pause; the CHP crisis gave it a reason to prioritize other fights. But Öcalan's continued imprisonment is not just a detainee issue — it is the procedural prerequisite the PKK attached to its own dissolution. Without movement there, the disarmament mandate the government celebrates has no legal force within the PKK's own framework.

sections.heading: Erdoğan eyes Trump meeting at World Cup match as NATO summit preparations intensify
sections.body:
Multiple sources told Middle East Eye that Erdoğan is seeking to arrange a meeting with Trump around the Turkey-US World Cup soccer match in Los Angeles on June 25. Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan said Erdoğan has spoken with Trump multiple times in the past month, and that in none of those calls did Trump suggest he would not attend the Ankara NATO summit on July 7-8.

Trump told Erdoğan in a May 20 phone call that he plans to attend the summit, reversing earlier signals that he was 'disgusted' with the alliance and considering withdrawal. Secretary of State Marco Rubio confirmed Trump will attend. European officials are now treating the Ankara summit as potentially the most important NATO gathering in decades, given the U.S. weighing troop withdrawals from Europe.

One European official told MEE: 'If he comes, there may be mayhem and shouting matches. However, if he doesn't come, it would be detrimental to the future of the alliance.' Another diplomat said the best outcome would be a roadmap for gradual U.S. withdrawal rather than an abrupt one.

The summit also serves as a defense industry showcase. Turkish officials say the sector will be a focal point, and the planned NATO defense industry forum will be the alliance's most comprehensive yet, according to Rutte.
sections.whyItMatters: A World Cup sideline meeting would give Erdoğan face time with Trump before the summit, potentially smoothing over the awkward fact that Turkey refused to host U.S. bases during the Iran war. The NATO summit's importance has escalated precisely because the alliance's future is in question — and Turkey, as host, gets to set the stage.

bottomLine: May's 32.6% inflation reading confirms the disinflation story has stalled — not reversed, but stuck well above the central bank's year-end target, with markets now pricing a hike to 40% before June ends. The big picture story today is the Reuters deep-dive on defense exports: $10 billion last year, triple since 2021, with a doubling target and 65% global armed-drone market share. KILIÇ underwater drones and Syria's first training abroad at Efes show that Turkey's military reach is diversifying in both domain and geography. Meanwhile, the PKK peace process is drifting — Karayılan's 'frozen' assessment directly contradicts Erdoğan, and the Öcalan condition remains the unmet prerequisite that neither side can bypass. The NATO summit in four weeks and a potential Trump-Erdoğan World Cup meeting in three weeks are the next waypoints.
sources: Reuters (inflation data, defense exports analysis, PKK peace process), RTE (inflation details), TUIK (official inflation statistics), Bloomberg (rate hike bets, Iran war budget impact), JPMorgan via Reuters/MSN (rate hike forecast), Naval News (KILIĆ underwater drones), Israel Defense (EFES-2026 exercise, Syria/Libya participation), Middle East Eye (Trump NATO summit, World Cup meeting), Al-Monitor (CHP court ruling, political crisis), AP via Newsday (PKK peace process frozen), Anadolu Agency via TRT World (Azerbaijan denies CNN report on Israeli operations), Defense News (Turkey Europe defense role), Hudson Institute (Kasapoglu defense industry analysis)
